title: Jelly Cupboard
summary: A classic farmhouse jelly cupboard — tall, shallow, two plank doors, 1x10 shelves set back for door clearance, with an optional tapered-leg face frame.
description:
The jelly cupboard is a piece with a résumé: tall and shallow because it stood against pantry walls, sized for rows of preserve jars, doors to keep the light off the jelly. This build keeps the classic silhouette in modern lumber — 1x12 sides, 1x10 shelves, a 1x3 face frame, plank-and-backing doors, and a 1/4" plywood or beadboard back (beadboard, if you're deciding, is what sells the era).

The dimensional trick that makes the doors work is in step 2 and easy to miss: the shelves are 1x10 in a 1x12 case, installed FLUSH TO THE BACK. That leaves about two inches of clearance behind the face frame — room for the doors' 1/4" backing panels to close into, and a shadow gap that keeps jar lids from fouling the doors. Use 1x12 shelves 'to fix it' and the doors will bounce off the shelving forever.

Door sizing is the other measure-don't-trust moment, and the source says why: BOARD WIDTHS VARY. A '1x12' can run 11-1/8" to 11-1/4" depending on the mill, so the door opening is whatever YOUR face frame produced. Measure it, then build each door 1/8" small on all sides. Hang on your hinges, add knobs and magnetic clasps — magnets, because a jelly cupboard's doors get opened with full hands.

The optional tapered feet (cut into the face frame's bottom rail with a jigsaw) are the one flourish; they lighten the whole piece visually for five minutes of cutting. This cupboard is tall and shallow, which is the tip-over geometry — strap it to a stud, especially if it will actually hold forty pounds of preserves.

tools.note: The plywood back and beadboard door panels.
The optional tapered feet in the face frame's bottom rail.

materials.name: 1x10, 8 ft
materials.note: Shelves — narrower than the case ON PURPOSE, for door-backing clearance.

materials.name: Plywood or beadboard, 1/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Back and door panels. Beadboard is the period-correct choice.

materials.name: Magnetic clasps
materials.note: Magnets, because these doors get opened with a jar in each hand.

materials.name: Anti-tip strap
materials.note: Tall + shallow = the tipping shape. Strap it to a stud.

cutList.note: Installed flush to the BACK — the 2" front gap is the door-backing clearance.
Optional tapered feet get cut into this.
Nominal — doors are built to YOUR openings minus 1/8" per side.

steps.title: Build the case
steps.body: Join the sides, bottom, and top with pocket screws and glue — mark the top's overhangs and set them evenly before driving anything. Check square by diagonals; a cupboard case that starts out a parallelogram delivers that parallelogram to the doors later, with interest.

steps.title: Install the shelves — flush to the BACK
steps.body:
Fit the three 1x10 shelves flush with the case's back edge. The ~2" gap this leaves at the front is not sloppiness — it's where the doors' backing panels swing in when they close, plus finger room for jar retrieval.

Space the shelves for actual jars: pints want about 6" clear, quarts about 8".

steps.title: Back it
steps.body: Square the case one more time, then nail the 1/4" back on with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue into every fixed board — sides, top, bottom, and all three shelves. Nailed to everything, the back is the case's shear panel; nailed to just the edges, it's a rattle.

steps.title: Face frame — and the optional feet
steps.body: Pocket-screw the face frame flat (stiles, top rail, bottom rail), then glue and nail it to the case front. Want the classic look? Draw a gentle taper at each end of the bottom rail and cut it with a jigsaw before attaching — instant feet, and the piece stops looking like a box.

steps.title: Build the doors to YOUR openings
steps.body:
Measure each door opening off the real face frame — board widths vary mill to mill, so the plan's numbers are estimates and your tape is the truth. Build each door frame 1/8" smaller than the opening on all sides, then nail and glue the 1/4" backing panel on (beadboard face out, if using).

Hang with hinges, mount the knobs, and set the magnetic clasps so the doors close with a satisfying period-correct thunk.

steps.title: Finish and anchor
steps.body:
Sand, then paint or stain — milk-paint colors (barn red, deep green) are the traditional move, distressed edges optional and forgivable.

Strap the cupboard to a wall stud before loading it: tall, shallow, and forty pounds of preserves behind two doors is the exact geometry tip-over warnings were written about. Then fill it with jam you made or store jam you'll claim you made.

images.alt: Tall farmhouse jelly cupboard with two plank doors
